7. 8. Urges States and other relevant actors to consider strengthening multisectoral child protection systems to prevent trafficking in girls and violence in all its forms, and to ensure comprehensive support for girls who are at risk of, or have been subjected to, violence, harassment, exploitation and abuse, including on the Internet, as well as harmful practices such as child marriage, early or forced marriage and genital mutilation, while paying particular attention to girls with disabilities and girls in vulnerable situations, including girls from indigenous communities and those suffering social or economic marginalization, including in rural and remote areas;

9. Recognizes that, in order to ensure equal and equitable access to quality education for all, education systems must be transformed, taking into account the gender perspective in school curricula, infrastructure development and teacher training, and in this regard calls upon States to invest in quality education, including through adequate funding, so that all girls, including those who are marginalized or in vulnerable situations, enjoy their right to education;

10. Calls upon Member States to work with relevant stakeholders, including the private sector and civil society, to take measures to bridge the digital divide, including the digital gender divide, as appropriate, among and within countries, in efforts to ensure the empowerment and safety of all young women and girls, including those living in rural and remote areas, including by providing distance learning, in particular in developing countries;

11. Stresses the importance of the right to education and further learning for all girls, recognizing that adolescent girls and girls with disabilities are at particular risk of dropping out of school during the VOCID-19 pandemic and not returning even after the reopening of schools, thereby becoming more vulnerable to poverty, the risk of child marriage and early or forced marriage, violence and early pregnancy;

items_en: 13. Notes the role of the United Nations in assisting national Governments in the implementation of the 2030 21 Agenda for Sustainable Development and in realizing the right of girls to education;

14. Calls upon States to recognize the right to education based on equal opportunities and non-discrimination by making primary education compulsory and free for all children, including in rural areas, and by ensuring that all children have equitable access to quality education and that secondary and higher education is accessible to all, in particular through the progressive introduction of free secondary education, bearing in mind that special measures to ensure equal access, including positive discrimination, unhindered access to education, including through increased financial incentives for families, the improvement of the safety of girls on their way to school, measures to ensure that all schools are safe and secure and free from violence, and the provision of health facilities in accordance with hygiene rules, separate and adequate, contribute to ensuring equal opportunities and combating exclusion, as well as to encouraging school attendance, in particular of girls and children from low-income families or heads of families;

15. Calls upon all States to place greater emphasis on quality education for girls, including by providing remedial and literacy courses to those who have not had formal education and by taking special initiatives to ensure that girls, including those who are already married or pregnant, continue their education after primary school, to promote access to entrepreneurial skills and training for young women, and to combat gender stereotypes to ensure that young women entering the labour market have access to full and productive employment, decent work and equal pay for equal work or work of equal value;

16. Encourages States to promote lifelong learning opportunities for all, by eliminating female illiteracy and promoting the acquisition of financial and digital skills, ensuring that girls have access to training in leadership, career development, scholarships and research grants, on an equal footing with boys, to strive to ensure that all children complete high-quality pre-school, primary and secondary education, to develop vocational and technical education for all girls, and to promote intercultural and multilingual education for all children, as appropriate, at all levels, in order to ensure that all children have access to high-quality pre-school, primary and secondary education.

items_en: 17. Also encourages States to adopt, where necessary, and implement inclusive policies and programmes to promote girls' education in science, technology, engineering and mathematics, including information technology, throughout their schooling, including by providing girls with more opportunities for training, from learning basic digital tools to acquiring advanced technical skills, recognizing that girls who acquire such skills are likely to achieve better academic performance and subsequently gain access to better paid jobs, and that women and girls have as important a role as men and boys in these areas;

18. Calls upon States, with the assistance of international organizations, civil society organizations and non-governmental organizations, as appropriate, to develop policies and programmes giving priority to formal and informal education that are comprehensive, scientifically accurate and age-appropriate and culturally sensitive, in order to provide adolescents and young people of both sexes, both in and out of school, with information that takes into account their evolving capacities, parents and legal guardians providing appropriate guidance and advice on sexual and reproductive health, HIV prevention, gender equality and the empowerment of women, human rights, physical and psychological development, puberty and power relations in relations between women and men, with a view to strengthening their self-esteem, developing their capacity to make informed decisions, communicate and control risks and fostering respectful relationships, in close partnership with young people, their parents, their guardians, those who care for them, educators and health-care providers, in order to protect themselves from, inter alia, from and from HIV infection;

19. Urges States to take into account the different needs of girls and boys during childhood and adolescence and to take appropriate measures, as appropriate, to respond to their changing circumstances, in particular by ensuring that girls have access to safe water, including safe drinking water, hygiene and sanitation services, women's hygiene items and, in educational and other public places, places of convenience to ensure their privacy, including the provision of receptacles for women's hygiene items, in order to improve their health and access to education and to enhance their safety;

20. Calls upon States, in collaboration with civil society and other relevant actors, to promote educational and health practices conducive to a culture in which menstruation is considered healthy and natural and does not lead to the stigmatization of girls, bearing in mind that girls' school attendance may be hampered by negative perceptions of menstruation and the unavailability in schools of means to enable girls to maintain their personal hygiene without risk, i.e. health facilities that are tailored to their needs;

21. Urges States to intensify their efforts to eliminate urgently all forms of discrimination against women and girls, and calls upon all States, the United Nations system and civil society to take steps to overcome the obstacles that continue to undermine the achievement of the goals set out in the Beijing Platform for Action 22 and identified in paragraph 33 of the Beijing Platform for Action,

24. items_en: 26. Urges States to adopt, where necessary, and to implement appropriate legislation to provide assistance, protection and empowerment to children living in child-headed households, in particular girls, that contain provisions to ensure the physical, psychosocial and economic well-being of such children, including by protecting their property and inheritance rights, and their access to health services, nutrition, safe water, including safe drinking water, sanitation and hygiene, housing, education, scholarships and training, as well as provisions to protect and assist their families in remaining together, including social protection and economic support initiatives, as appropriate;

27. Also urges States to develop partnerships with relevant stakeholders, in particular by working with the local population to develop programmes and mechanisms to ensure the safety, protection and empowerment of children, especially girls, and to ensure that they receive the support they need from their communities;

28. Calls upon States to support the research, collection and analysis of data on girls, disaggregated by household structure, sex, age, disability, economic status, marital status, migration status, geographical origin and other relevant criteria at the national level, and to improve gender-disaggregated statistics on time use, unpaid care and water and sanitation, with a view to enhancing understanding of the situations of girls, in particular the multiple forms of discrimination against them, and to provide support for the development of policies and programmes necessary to address them, by adopting a comprehensive gender-sensitive approach, appropriate to the age of the beneficiaries and aimed at combating all forms of discrimination against girls, in order to promote, respect, protect and effectively realize their rights;

29. Urges States to take all necessary measures to ensure to girls with disabilities the full and equal enjoyment of all human rights and fundamental freedoms on an equal footing with other children, and to adopt and implement policies and programmes that meet their needs, or to strengthen those policies and programmes;

30. Urges all States to adopt and enforce laws protecting girls from all forms of violence, discrimination and exploitation and from all harmful practices, regardless of the circumstances, including infanticide and fetal selection by sex, female genital mutilation, rape, domestic violence, incest, sexual abuse and exploitation, child prostitution, child pornography and other material showing sexual violence against children, trafficking and forced migration, forced labour, child marriages and early and forced marriages, and to develop and implement laws that protect girls from all forms of violence, discrimination and exploitation and against all harmful practices, regardless of the circumstances, including child and sex-related selection of the foetus, child prostitution, child pornography and other material showing sexual violence against children, trafficking and forced migration, forced labour, child marriages and early and forced marriages.

items_en: 31. Urges States to strengthen and intensify efforts to prevent and eliminate all forms of violence against girls in schools, and to ensure that perpetrators of such violence are held accountable;

32. Calls upon all States, in cooperation with stakeholders, including the private sector and the media, to adopt and enforce the legislative or other measures necessary to prevent the dissemination on the Internet of child pornography and other material showing sexual violence against children, ensuring that adequate mechanisms are in place to enable the presence of such content to be reported and withdrawn and that the perpetrators, distributors and holders of such content are prosecuted, as appropriate;

33. Urges States to develop comprehensive, multidisciplinary and coordinated plans, programmes or strategies to eliminate all forms of violence and discrimination against women and girls, which should be accompanied by special resources and widely disseminated, to set targets and time frames and to provide for concrete internal implementation procedures, involving monitoring and evaluation mechanisms that involve all relevant stakeholders, including consultations with women's organizations, or to review existing ones, as appropriate, paying attention to the recommendations on girls made by the Special Rapporteur on violence against women, its causes and consequences and the Special Rapporteur on trafficking in persons, especially women and children, of the Human Rights Council, and by the Special Representative of the Secretary-General on violence against children;

34. Also urges States to ensure that children capable of forming their own views have the right to express them freely on all matters affecting them and that such views are duly taken into account in accordance with the age and maturity of the children issuing them, to ensure that this right is fully exercised by girls on an equal basis, to actively involve girls, including those with special needs and those with disabilities, as well as organizations representing them, in decision-making, as appropriate, and to involve them as full partners in the identification of their own needs and in the formulation, planning, implementation and evaluation of policies and programmes to address them, with a view to ensuring their full and effective participation;

35. Recognizes that a significant number of girls are particularly vulnerable, be they orphans, street children, internally displaced or refugee children, victims of trafficking and sexual and economic exploitation, living with HIV and AIDS or affected by the disease, or incarcerated or without parental support, and therefore urges States to take appropriate measures to address the needs of such children with the support, as appropriate, of the international community, through national, regional and subregional policies and strategies to strengthen the capacity of Governments, communities and families to provide them with appropriate counselling and psychosocial support, ensure their safety and schooling and ensure that they have equal access to housing, nutrition and social and health services;

36. Urges all States and the international community to respect, promote and protect the rights of girls, taking into account the particular vulnerability of girls before, during and after conflict, in the context of climate and other hazards in the event of natural disasters and other humanitarian emergencies, and urges States to take special measures to ensure the protection of girls, at all stages of humanitarian response, from relief to recovery, and in particular to ensure that children have access to basic services, including safe water, including safe drinking water, sanitation and hygiene, to protect them from sexually transmitted infections, including HIV infection, gender-based violence, including rape, sexual abuse and exploitation, torture, abduction and trafficking, including forced labour, with special attention to refugees and internally displaced persons, and to take into account their special needs during disarmament, demobilization, rehabilitation and reintegration processes;

37. Calls upon States to take into account the perspectives and priorities of girls, including those living in rural and remote areas or in poverty, in armed conflict and post-conflict situations and in humanitarian emergencies, and to ensure their full and effective participation in the design, implementation, monitoring and evaluation of policies, programmes and activities related to conflict prevention, peace mediation, peace-building and post-conflict reconstruction, in accordance with their age and maturity, recognizing the contributions of all members of society in raising awareness about the stigmatization of children, including girls affected by armed conflict;

38. Deplores all acts of sexual exploitation, abuse and trafficking of women and children, including in situations of humanitarian crisis, including those involving humanitarian workers and peacekeepers, including military, police and civilians involved in United Nations operations, takes note of the Secretary-General's optional compact on prevention of and response to sexual exploitation and abuse, welcomes the efforts of the United Nations system and peacekeeping operations to impose a zero-tolerance policy in this regard, and requests the Secretary-General or Member States of which such humanitarian workers are nationals and troop-contributing countries to continue to take all necessary measures to combat acts of violence and exploitation committed by such personnel, including by ensuring the full and expeditious implementation of the measures adopted by the General Assembly in its resolutions on the basis of the recommendations of the Special Committee on Peacekeeping Operations; 24

39. Calls upon Member States to formulate, enforce and strengthen child and youth-centred measures to combat, eliminate and suppress all forms of trafficking in women and girls, including for sexual and economic exploitation, as part of a comprehensive strategy to combat trafficking that would be part of the broader effort to eliminate all forms of violence against women and girls, including by taking effective measures against the criminalization of girls who are victims of exploitation and by ensuring that those who have been exploited receive the necessary psychosocial support, and in this regard urges Member States, the United Nations and other international, regional and subregional organizations, as well as civil society, including non-governmental organizations, the private sector and the international community, to ensure that all forms of trafficking in women and girls are addressed in a comprehensive strategy to combat, eradicate and punish all forms of trafficking in women and girls, including for the purpose of sexual and economic exploitation.
